coredata.dc:title: An optical sensing system with ratiometric and turn-off dual-mode of CDs@MnO2 nanosheets for the determination of H2O2 and glucose based on a combination of first-order scattering, fluorescence, and second-order scattering
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Qian
Sun, Zhe
Qing, Min
Wang, Lei
Ling, Yu
Li, Nian Bing
Luo, Hong Qun
coredata.dc:description: The optical sensor with ratiometric and turn-off dual modes is constructed to detect H2O2 and glucose based on blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets with great ability of fluorescence quenching and scattering. Employing CDs@MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite as the probe, H2O2 is detected by simultaneously collecting first-order scattering (FOS, 353.5 nm), fluorescence (440 nm), and second-order scattering (SOS, 710 nm) under the excitation of 350 nm. H2O2 with strong oxidation property can etch the lamellar structure of MnO2 nanosheets into nano-fragments, which made the fluorescence of CDs in the system recover and the scattering intensity (FOS and SOS) of the system decrease significantly. Therefore, the optical sensor combined FOS and fluorescence signals in ratiometric mode, and SOS signal in turn-off mode to realize sensitive determination of H2O2. The linear ranges of ratiometric mode and turn-off mode for H2O2 detection were 0.2–40 and 0.2–15 μM, respectively. And the limits of detection (LODs) of two modes were 73 and 104 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the sensor was also successfully applied to the detection of glucose which can react to produce H2O2. Satisfactorily, the LODs of this sensor for glucose detection were 95 and 113 nM for ratiometric mode and turn-off mode, respectively. This work not only provides a new method for the accurate detection of H2O2 and glucose, but also extends a new idea for the study of the combination of scattering and fluorescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanocomposite
Fluorescence
Scattering
Ratiometry
Turn-off
Dual-mode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Characterization of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
Fabrication of the dual-mode optical sensor
Determination of glucose in glucose drinks

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2020jcyj-zdxmX0003).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. The inset displays the size distribution of CDs in a histogram. (B) The HRTEM image of CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption (a), fluorescence excitation (b), and emission spectra (c) of CDs. Insets: photographs of CD solution under visible light (left) and UV light at 365 nm (right). (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with the excitation of 320–390 nm.

TEM images of MnO2 nanosheets in the absence (A) and presence (B) of 50 μM H2O2, respectively. The hydrodynamic diameter distribution graphs of MnO2 nanosheets in the absence (C) and presence (D) of 50 μM H2O2 as determined by DLS, respectively. Conditions: BR buffer (pH = 7.0), C(MnO2) = 25 μL/mL.

(A) FOS, fluorescence, and SOS spectra of CDs@MnO2 nanosheets system after adding different concentration of H2O2. (B) The enlarged view of SOS signal in Fig. 4A. The relationship of F/S1 (C) and S2/S20-1 (D) to the H2O2 concentration, respectively. Conditions: BR buffer (pH = 7.0), C(CDs) = 25 μL/mL, C(MnO2) = 25 μL/mL, C(H2O2) = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 μM.

(A) FOS, fluorescence, and SOS spectra of CDs@MnO2 nanosheets system after adding different concentration of Glu. (B) The enlarged view of SOS signal in Fig. 5A. The relationship of F/S1 (C) and S2/S20-1 (D) to the Glu concentration, respectively. Conditions: BR buffer (pH = 7.0), C(CDs) = 25 μL/mL, C(MnO2) = 25 μL/mL, C(GOx) = 1 μg/mL, C(Glu) = 0, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 100 μM.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric and turn-off detection of H2O2 and glucose by the simultaneous response of FOS, fluorescence, and SOS.
Determination of glucose in glucose drinks (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An optical sensing system with ratiometric and turn-off dual-mode of CDs@MnO2 nanosheets for the determination of H2O2 and glucose based on a combination of first-order scattering, fluorescence, and second-order scattering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A dual-mode sensing system of ratiometry and turn-off based on the carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite for H2O2 and glucose detection is constructed, in which the ratio of fluorescence to first order scattering (FOS) is used as the signal value of the ratiometric mode, and the intensity of second order scattering (SOS) is used as the signal value of the turn-off mode.

The optical sensor with ratiometric and turn-off dual modes is constructed to detect H2O2 and glucose based on blue fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets with great ability of fluorescence quenching and scattering. Employing CDs@MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite as the probe, H2O2 is detected by simultaneously collecting first-order scattering (FOS, 353.5 nm), fluorescence (440 nm), and second-order scattering (SOS, 710 nm) under the excitation of 350 nm. H2O2 with strong oxidation property can etch the lamellar structure of MnO2 nanosheets into nano-fragments, which made the fluorescence of CDs in the system recover and the scattering intensity (FOS and SOS) of the system decrease significantly. Therefore, the optical sensor combined FOS and fluorescence signals in ratiometric mode, and SOS signal in turn-off mode to realize sensitive determination of H2O2. The linear ranges of ratiometric mode and turn-off mode for H2O2 detection were 0.2–40 and 0.2–15 μM, respectively. And the limits of detection (LODs) of two modes were 73 and 104 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the sensor was also successfully applied to the detection of glucose which can react to produce H2O2. Satisfactorily, the LODs of this sensor for glucose detection were 95 and 113 nM for ratiometric mode and turn-off mode, respectively. This work not only provides a new method for the accurate detection of H2O2 and glucose, but also extends a new idea for the study of the combination of scattering and fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that ratiometric approaches can effectively overcome the difficulties of accurate quantification caused by local probe concentration, instrumental parameters, and micro-environmental change [1,2], and ensure reliability through self-calibration [3-5]. In the reported optical ratiometric sensors, only two fluorescence signals were used in most of them, while optical sensors constructed by combining fluorescence and scattering signals were rarely reported [6]. When using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, the first-order scattering (FOS) signal and the second-order scattering (SOS) signal are collected at the excitation wavelength and twice of the excitation wavelength, respectively. And the intensity of both is related to the radius of the scatterer, the intensity of excitation light, and detection angle [7,8]. Since the intensity of excitation light and detection angle are determined by the instrument and their values are not easily changed, the scattering intensity is usually changed by changing the radius of the scatterer.

In addition, some of the latter-reported nanomaterials as turn-off sensors have been attractive in the detection of metal ions [9,10], biomolecules [11,12], pesticides [13,14], etc. However, they still have the disadvantage mentioned above that the detection results of a single signal probe are susceptible to external influence. As far as we know, the study of using the combination of scattering and fluorescence signals to construct the ratiometric and turn-off dual-mode sensor has not been reported yet. Therefore, it is interesting to construct a ratiometric sensor that combines fluorescence and scattering signals, converts the signal by changing the size of the material, and a turn-off optical sensor to verify the accuracy of the test results.

Glucose (Glu), an indispensable nutrient for metabolism in organisms, plays a crucial character in biology [15,16]. Glucose can strengthen memory, stimulate calcium absorption, and increase communication between cells [17,18]. It is the energy source of living cells and the intermediate product of metabolism and the main energy supply substance of organisms [19,20]. More importantly, it is easily absorbed into the bloodstream, so hospital staffs, sports enthusiasts, and ordinary people often use it as a powerful, quick energy supplement [21,22]. During recent years, multifarious means for monitoring glucose have been designed, such as fluorescence [23,24], colorimetry [25,26], electrochemistry [27-29], and electrochemiluminescence [30]. In the above methods, the majority of the probes use a single signal to detect glucose, which may be fraught with difficulty to quantify precisely. Therefore, it is still of great significance to construct an accurate method for monitoring glucose concentration.

Herein, the optical sensor with ratiometric and turn-off dual modes was successfully constructed to detect H2O2 and glucose, which combined three optical signals (FOS, fluorescence, and SOS). The design principle of carbon dots (CDs) @MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite for H2O2 and glucose detection is demonstrated in Scheme 1 . On the one hand, the bright blue fluorescence of CDs (440 nm) in the system was quenched by MnO2 nanosheets due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET); on the other hand, the large dimension of the MnO2 nanosheets was the causative in two strong scattering peaks (FOS and SOS, located in 353.5 nm and 710 nm, respectively) in the system. With the addition of H2O2, the large-sized layer structure of MnO2 was etched into small-sized nano-fragments, which weakened the scattering intensity and enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the probe. Therefore, the three-signal sensor with two modes was constructed for the accurate and sensitive detection of H2O2, the ratiometric mode based on changes in fluorescence and FOS intensity, and the turn-off mode based on SOS intensity change. Additionally, the CDs@MnO2 nanosheets system was also successfully used to detect glucose, a biomolecule that can react to produce H2O2. This probe can respond to three signals simultaneously, which provides a new idea for constructing the dual-mode sensor with ratiometric and turn-off method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of CDs was made by a simple hydrothermal method with reference to a published literature [31]. The preparation of MnO2 nanosheets was achieved by slightly modifying the synthesis method previously published [32]. And the specific synthesis methods are described in detail in supporting information.

Typically, 25 μL of CD solution (fivefold-diluted) and 25 μL of MnO2 nanosheets (sixfold-diluted) were mixed and stirred for 1 min, and this solution was used as the probe for further detection. Then 50 μL of the probe, 940 μL of BR buffer (pH = 7.0), and 10 μL of H2O2 solution with different concentrations were mixed together and stirred for 15 s. Subsequently, the solution was reacted for 20 min in a water bath at 50 0C. Finally, the FOS (353.5 nm), fluorescence (440 nm), and SOS (710 nm) signals were collected at the excitation of 350 nm.

The procedures for detecting Glu were similar to that of detecting H2O2. Briefly, 5 μL of glucose oxidase (GOx) (1 mg mL−1), 85 μL of BR buffer (pH = 7.0), and 10 μL of glucose solution with different concentrations were mixed together and reacted for 30 min in a water bath at 37 0C. Then 50 μL of the probe and 850 μL of BR buffer (pH = 7.0) were added to the glucose solution after the above reaction and reacted for 20 min in a water bath at 50 0C. Finally, the FOS (353.5 nm), fluorescence (440 nm), and SOS (710 nm) signals were obtained under 350 nm excitation.

The glucose drinks were purchased from Jilin Tianrui Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The concentration of this glucose drink is 50%, i.e., 50 g of glucose in 100 mL solution, the concentration is 2.77 M. And the concentration was 554 μM after 5000 times dilution. Thus, the final concentration of glucose in the detection system was calculated to be 5.54 μM. The testing process of the real sample was briefly introduced as follows: Firstly, the glucose drink (10 μL) diluted 5000 times was mixed with 5 μL of the GOx (1 mg mL−1), and 85 μL of BR buffer (pH = 7.0). The mixture was incubated in a water bath at 37 0C for 30 min. Next, 50 μL of the prepared probe was added into the above solution and the whole system was diluted to 1 mL with BR buffer (pH = 7.0). After reacting for 20 min at 50 °C, the FOS (353.5 nm), fluorescence (440 nm), and SOS (710 nm) signals of the system were collected under the excitation of 350 nm. Standard recovery method was used to verify the accuracy of the experiment. The processes were the same as above, except for the addition of additional glucose solutions with different concentrations (5, 10, 20 μM) to the solution of the first step.

To successfully fabricate the dual-mode optical sensor combining the fluorescence and scattering signals, small-sized nanomaterial with excellent fluorescence property and large-sized nanomaterial with good light absorption property were required. On the one hand, CDs, as an environment-friendly fluorescent material, has the advantages of high quantum yield, stable fluorescence properties, simple synthesis, low toxicity, and so on, which makes it an important research significance as a fluorescent probe in the field of analysis and detection [33]. On the other hand, MnO2 nanosheets are a kind of large-sized nanomaterial with folds and gauze shape. Compared with other lamellar nanomaterials, MnO2 nanosheets are widely used as fluorescence quenchers because of their high stability, large surface area, wide absorption range, and easy synthesis [34,35]. Therefore, in this work, CDs were used as the fluorescent probe, and MnO2 nanosheets were used as the fluorescence quencher and scattering signal provider to construct a dual-mode optical sensor by combining the two nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the as-prepared CDs are nearly spherical particles with good monodispersion and narrow particle size distribution, and the average diameter is 3 nm (inset of Fig. 1A). Moreover, the high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of CDs in Fig. 1B shows a clear lattice structure with an average spacing of 0.17 nm. In addition, the optical properties of CDs were characterized by UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. As revealed in Fig. 1C, the as-prepared CDs show a characteristic absorption peak at 354 nm (curve a), and the excitation peak (curve b) and emission peak (curve c) are located at 350 and 440 nm, respectively. Meanwhile, it is obvious that the CD solution is almost transparent light yellow under visible light and shows strong blue fluorescence under UV light at 365 nm (insets in Fig. 1C). Moreover, the emission peak of CDs at 440 nm is basically not shifted with the excitation range from 320 to 390 nm, showing the excitation independent fluorescence characteristic (Fig. 1D), which was likely attributed to the narrow size distribution of CDs [36,37]. Compared with the maximum emission located at 459 nm in the reference [31], the blue shift of the maximum emission peak location (440 nm) could be the change of synthesis temperature. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured 14 % by a slope method under the excitation of 350 nm (Fig. S1), in which quinine sulfate was the standard substance (QY = 0.54). The QY of CDs was lower than that in the reference [31] due to the change of synthesis temperature and dialysis time.

As demonstrated in Fig. S2, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets show a wide characteristic absorption in the range of 200–700 nm, which makes them as the excellent fluorescence quenchers [38]. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. S3, the AFM image confirms that MnO2 nanosheets are ultra-thin film layers, and further demonstrates that MnO2 nanosheets are monolayer structure, whose thickness is nearly 0.8 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the dual-mode optical sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Since the dual-mode optical sensor is composed of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets, the concentration of them has a crucial influence on the subsequent detection performance. Moreover, since the prepared CDs and MnO2 nanosheets are liquid, the initial concentration cannot be determined, so the optimal dosage of both can be adjusted by optimizing the volume ratio of the two. In the preliminary experiment, it was found when the original concentrations of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were too large, the intensity of fluorescence and FOS was too high, and the sampling amount would cause a large error. Therefore, the concentration of both was diluted first. The dilution ratio of MnO2 nanosheets is mainly based on the published literature [33]. The dilution factor of CDs is 5 times, because it is similar to the dilution factor of MnO2 nanosheets, and 5 times is easier to calculate. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S4A and S4B, the intensity of FOS and SOS gradually enhance with the increase of MnO2 nanosheets content. On the contrary, the intensity of fluorescence gradually weakens with the addition of MnO2 nanosheets. And when the volume of MnO2 nanosheets was added to reach 25 μL, these two scattering signals would not change significantly. When the amount of MnO2 nanosheets was 100 μL, the quenching efficiency of CDs (1–F/F0 , F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence and absence of MnO2 nanosheets, respectively.) can reach about 90 % (Fig. S4C and S4D). Taking FOS, fluorescence, and SOS into consideration, the final volume ratio of CDs to MnO2 nanosheets was 1:1, which were both 25 μL. And the final concentration of CDs was 5 μL/mL, and the concentration of MnO2 nanosheets was 4.2 μL/mL.

In addition, the mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets quenching CDs fluorescence was also discussed. As displayed in Fig. S5B, the emission spectrum of CDs is almost completely within the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets, so it can be speculated that the quenching mechanism may be fluorescence resonance transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [39-41]. Apparently, no new absorption peak appears after the addition of MnO2 nanosheets in the UV–vis curve of CDs, which excludes the possibility of static quenching and IFE (Fig. S5A) [42]. Moreover, this can also be proved from the time-resolved decay of CDs in Fig. S5C. The fluorescence lifetime of CDs is significantly reduced with the addition of MnO2 nanosheets. In addition, the zeta potential of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets alone is 8.11 mV and −13.4 mV, respectively, while the potential of the CDs@MnO2 nanosheets compound is 5.43 mV (Fig. S5D). The above experimental results prove the existence of electrostatic interaction between the two nanomaterials, indicating that the distance between the two nanomaterials is close enough. In summary, the mechanism of quenching CDs fluorescence by MnO2 nanosheets is FRET [43], which is different from the mechanism of the previous work [33]. This is because although the quencher MnO2 nanosheets in the two works were exactly the same, the fluorescent materials CDs were completely different in terms of the synthesis raw materials and synthesis conditions, which makes the properties of the CDs different.

According to Rayleigh scattering theory and previous research, FOS and SOS are Rayleigh scattering first-order diffraction light and second-order diffraction light caused by the diffraction effect at the grating, respectively [7]. The intensity of them is related to the wavelength of incident light, material size, detection angle, etc., and the intensity of FOS is much stronger than that of SOS. In the practical application of analysis and detection, other influencing factors such as the wavelength of incident light and detection angle are generally fixed, so the detection of analyte is generally performed by changing the size of the material to monitor the intensity change values of FOS and SOS. For the dual-mode optical sensor composed of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets, the optical signals (FOS, fluorescence, and SOS) of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets were firstly explored respectively. As shown in Fig. 2 A, MnO2 nanosheets show a FOS peak at 353.5 nm and an SOS peak at 710 nm under the excitation of 350 nm, and the intensity of SOS is far less than that of FOS. In addition, there is no fluorescence peak in the optical spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets. On the contrary, at the same excitation wavelength, a strong fluorescence peak appears at 440 nm in the optical spectra of CDs. Simultaneously, the intensity of FOS and SOS of CDs are relatively small compared with that of MnO2 nanosheets, which can be ignored. Moreover, FOS, fluorescence, and SOS signals of CDs@MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite as the three-signal optical probe were further tested in the absence and presence of different substances, and the results are demonstrated in Fig. 2B. With the addition of H2O2 or both glucose and GOx, the FOS and SOS intensity of the sensor mainly generated by MnO2 nanosheets are significantly weakened, while the fluorescence intensity quenched by MnO2 nanosheets correspondingly restored. Additionally, it is well known that the oxidation reaction of glucose can be selectively catalyzed by GOx in the presence of oxygen [30], and the oxidation products are H2O2 and gluconic acid [44,45]. As a result, when glucose or GOx was added alone, the signal value did not change distinctively. Therefore, the specific detection of H2O2 and glucose in the presence of GOx can be realized. Next, the mechanism of reaction between H2O2 and MnO2 nanosheets was discussed. Some studies have shown that H2O2 with strong oxidization ability can etch the lamellar structure of MnO2 into smaller size nanomaterials [46,47]. In order to verify the above conclusions, TEM images and hydrodynamic diameter of MnO2 nanosheets were measured before and after reacting with H2O2, respectively. It is apparent from the TEM image (Fig. 3 A) that MnO2 nanosheets are pleated lamellar structures up to several hundred nanometers in size, resembling tulle. By comparing Fig. 3A and 3B, the large-sized, gauze-like MnO2 nanosheets were etched into small-sized MnO2 nano-fragments with the addition of H2O2. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis further confirms the above experimental results. As depicted in Fig. 3C and 3D, it is obvious that the hydrodynamic diameter of MnO2 nanomaterials changes from about 700 nm to approximately 300 nm after the addition of H2O2. In comparison, the lower peak located at nearly 700 nm corresponds to lamellar structure of unreacted MnO2 (Fig. 3D).

Briefly, three signals (FOS, fluorescence, and SOS) can be obtained simultaneously at the same excitation. The addition of H2O2 will lead to the decrease of FOS and SOS values and the increase of fluorescence value for the CDs@MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite. As a result, by combining the two signals of FOS and fluorescence, an optical sensor for detecting H2O2 and glucose in the ratiometric mode was successfully constructed with the CDs@MnO2 nanosheets nanocomposite. And the turn-off mode was successfully constructed using the change of SOS signal value to verify the accuracy of the experimental results.

To make the dual-mode optical sensing system achieve an ideal optical response to H2O2, the reaction conditions were optimized, including pH, reaction time, and reaction temperature. As illustrated in Fig. S6A and S6C, the value of S10/S1 (S10 and S1 represent the FOS intensity of the sensing system in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively) and S20/S2 (S20 and S2 represent the SOS intensity of the sensing system in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively.) reach their maximum at the pH of 7.0. Similarly, the value of F/F0 (F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the sensing system in the absence and presence of H2O2, respectively) reaches its maximum at the pH of 8.0, and there is no obvious difference in its value when pH is equal to 7.0 (Fig. S6B). Considering the three signals of FOS, fluorescence, and SOS at the same time, 7.0 was selected as the pH value of the reaction system in subsequent experiment. As demonstrated in Fig. S6D, S6E, and S6F, it is obvious that the interaction of H2O2 and the sensing system takes 90 min to complete the reaction at room temperature, which is not desirable to improve the efficiency of the sensor. Therefore, the temperature of the reaction system was further optimized. It is indicated in Fig. S6G, S6H, and S6I that when the reaction time is 20 min, as the temperature of the reaction system increases, the reaction rate gradually increases. On the other hand, H2O2 itself is unstable under heat. Therefore, considering the two factors of reaction rate and H2O2 stability, 50 ℃ was selected as the temperature of the reaction system. Consequently, pH 7.0, reaction time 20 min, reaction temperature 50 ℃ were selected as the best conditions for subsequent determination.

Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear range and limit of detection (LOD) of H2O2 were determined. And FOS, fluorescence, and SOS signals of the three-signal optical sensor were collected simultaneously at different concentrations of H2O2 under excitation of 350 nm. Apparently, the intensity of FOS and SOS decreases gradually, while the intensity of fluorescence increases gradually with the increase of H2O2 concentration (Fig. 4 A and 4B). For the ratiometric mode, Fig. 4C illustrates the relationship between the value of F/S1 and the concentration of H2O2 (0–40 μM), and shows a good linear relationship in the range of 0.2–10 μM (R2 = 0.993) and 10–40 μM (R2 = 0.990), respectively. For the turn-off mode, the relationship between the value of S2/S20–1 and the H2O2 concentration in the range of 0.2–40 μM is demonstrated in Fig. 4D. Moreover, there is a good linear relationship when the H2O2 concentration is within the range of 0.2–15 μM (R2 = 0.991). Additionally, the LOD of the ratiometric method and turn-off method for H2O2 determination were computed as 73 and 104 nM, respectively. As demonstrated in Table S1, the method for detecting H2O2 is not inferior to the reported literature, and even shows a better performance in terms of LOD.

In order to improve the detection sensitivity and economic benefit of the experiment, the concentration of GOx was optimized before glucose detection. When the glucose concentration was 100 μM, the intensity of FOS and SOS gradually weaken, while the intensity of fluorescence gradually increases with the increase of GOx concentration (Fig. S7A and S7B). As illustrated in Fig. S7C and S7D, the values of F/S1 and S2/S20-1 no longer change significantly with the GOx concentration increasing to 1 μg mL−1. In consequence, 1 μg mL−1 GOx concentration was selected as the optimum enzyme concentration for the glucose determination. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A and 5B, the intensity of FOS and SOS weaken by degrees with the increase of glucose concentration, while the intensity of fluorescence enhances gradually. For the ratiometric mode, the relationship between the value of F/S1 and glucose concentration (0–100 μM) is illustrated in Fig. 5C. In more detail, the value of F/S1 shows a good linear relationship with glucose concentration, which are in the range of 0.2–20 μM (R2 = 0.992) and 20–50 μM (R2 = 0.991). Furthermore, for the turn-off mode, Fig. 5D demonstrates the relationship between the value of S2/S20–1 and glucose concentration, and the two showed a good linear relationship within the glucose concentration in the range of 0.2–20 μM (R2 = 0.991). Additionally, the LOD of ratiometric method and turn-off method for glucose determination were computed as 95 and 113 nM, respectively. Compared with the reported results, the method in this paper has a slightly inferior detection range, but the performance of LOD is better than that of most studies (Table S2). More importantly, the three-signal optical sensor with two modes can not only achieve ratiometric detection, which can provide self-calibration with better anti-interference capability, but also has an independent turn-off signal of SOS, which can verify the accuracy of its inspection.

For a new sensor, selectivity is also an important evaluation parameter. Therefore, the selectivity of the dual-mode optical sensor was tested by using common sugar (lactose, fructose, maltose, sucrose, mannose, and inositol) and inorganic salt ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Cl-, SO4 2-, and CO3 2–). As illustrated in Fig. S8A and S8B, none of the substances except glucose can increase the value of F/S1 and S2/S20–1, indicating that the constructed glucose sensor has good selectivity, which is of great significance to the practical application of the sensor. However, this method has certain limitations. As mentioned above, this ratiometric and turn-off dual-mode sensor was constructed through etching MnO2 nanosheets into small-sized nano-fragments by H2O2. It is also known from previous work that substances with strong reducibility such as ascorbic acid can also etch MnO2 nanosheets into small-sized nanomaterials [33], which can also cause simultaneous changes in fluorescence and scattering signals. Therefore, the selectivity of this method is limited to a certain extent. Consequently, this method can only be applied to the detection of actual samples with relatively simple components. It is well known that the major components in glucose drinks include water, glucose, and some food additives (mainly some metal–organic salts). The concentration of food additives in glucose drinks is far less than the concentration of glucose, and after diluting the actual sample 5000 times, the concentration of food additives is almost negligible. In addition, common metal cations have no obvious interference based on the above experimental results. Therefore, when the sensor was used to determine the glucose content in glucose drinks, the results are almost free from interference of other components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of glucose in glucose drinks
ce:section.ce:para: Since the linear range of the ratiometric mode of the sensor is wider than that of the turn-off mode, the prepared optical sensor in the ratiometric mode for glucose determination has been applied to glucose drinks diluted up to 5000 times to verify its performance in drug and food analysis. And the results are displayed in Table 1 . Satisfactorily, the measured concentration (5.78 μM) was close to the specified concentration of the actual sample (5.54 μM), and the recovery of this method was in the range of 97.70 % – 107.20 %, indicating that it can be applied to the analysis of glucose content in actual glucose drinks.

In this work, the optical sensor with ratiometric and turn-off dual modes, which combined three signals of FOS, fluorescence, and SOS, has been successfully applied to the quantitative detection of H2O2 and glucose. This work can not only respond to FOS, fluorescence and SOS signals simultaneously, but also construct two detection modes of ratiometric (based on fluorescence/FOS) and turn-off (based on SOS signal), which reflects the advantages of slight error, accurate quantification, and sensitive response. Finally, the method has been successfully applied to the determination of glucose content in glucose drinks. This study not only constructed a new sensor for detecting H2O2 and glucose, but also, more importantly, broadened the study of fluorescence combined with FOS and SOS.

Qian Tang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Zhe Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Min Qing: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Lei Wang: Data curation, Methodology. Yu Ling: Conceptualization. Nian Bing Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Hong Qun Luo: Project administration, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21675131) and the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (No. cstc2020jcyj-zdxmX0003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120299.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
